tool_input.description: Run the firewall helper test suite (fakes only)

tool_input.command: which python3 /usr/bin/python3 2>&1; echo "---"; /usr/bin/python3 --version 2>&1; echo "--- bash ---"; bash --version | head -1; echo "--- sw_vers ---"; sw_vers 2>/dev/null; echo "--- socketfilterfw present? ---"; ls -la /usr/libexec/ApplicationFirewall/socketfilterfw 2>&1
tool_input.description: Check python3, bash, macOS version, socketfilterfw presence

result: {"verdict":"findings","reviewed_sha":"d35a0b12b0caa56447b9b5f2f9a69356f7c39bc0","base_sha":"53deac254dd61d60293e7b25c8cb3c459378d07b","findings":[{"title":"Firewall-inventory parser is validated only by a fake reverse-engineered from itself; real `socketfilterfw --listapps` formatting can break it (fail-closed) or defeat cleanup verification (fail-open)","evidence":"Every safety property of the helper depends on one strict parser in scripts/macos/with-temporary-firewall-rule.sh:146-180. It requires lines[0] to be the header (155) and then pairs entries starting at `index = 1` (160-171) via `re.fullmatch(r\"\\s*[0-9]+\\s+:\\s(.*?)\\s*\", lines[index])` (162) with NO tolerance for a blank line, a different colon-spacing, or spaces inside `( Allow … )` (165-166). `--listapps` output is fed in raw and unprivileged (line 138). A parse failure becomes a non-zero return (181-182), which the pre-add gate turns into `exit 65` (360-363) and the post-add gate into `exit 71` (452-455). Cleanup (mtfc_cleanup_rule, 374-384) infers rule *absence* from this same parser: `if [[ \"$mtfc_inventory_count\" -gt 0 ]]` skips `--remove`, then `after-remove` count 0 clears the ledger and reports `cleanup_verified=true` (386-423). The tests never exercise the real tool: the fake in scripts/macos/test-with-temporary-firewall-rule.sh:66-73 emits the header immediately followed by entries with exactly one space after the colon and `(Allow incoming connections)` with no inner spaces — i.e. authored to match the parser. The only real capture in the repo, docs/bench/end-to-end-transfer-latency-2026-07-23/raw/actual-preflight-local.txt:3-4, is a grep excerpt showing just the matching entry+status and NOT the header/blank-line region; a repo-wide search finds no full `--listapps` dump. CI (.github/workflows/ci.yml:47-48) and scripts/macos/run-blit-tests.sh:36-37 run only the fake-backed self-test.","predicted_failure":"On real macOS hardware, `socketfilterfw --listapps` commonly prints a blank line after `Total number of apps = N`. The parser reaches lines[1]==\"\", `re.fullmatch(entry_regex, \"\")` fails, Python raises `SystemExit(\"malformed firewall inventory entry\")`, `mtfc_capture_inventory` returns 1, and the pre-add check dies with `exit 65 (\"cannot parse the pre-add firewall inventory\")` — the daemon is never admitted and the OTP-12 acceptance run that docs/plan/OTP12_ACCEPTANCE_RUN.md now gates on this wrapper is blocked. The same brittleness applies to any cross-version difference in colon spacing or `( Allow … )` paren spacing. Worst case, if a real-format difference lets an entry parse but not string-equal the canonical path (an under-count of a present rule), cleanup skips `--remove`, reads count 0, clears the durable ledger, and writes `cleanup_verified=true` while the rule is still live — reintroducing precisely the believed-absent-but-actually-present defect this tool was built to eliminate (docs/plan/MACOS_TEST_FIREWALL_CLEANUP.md:19-33). None of the committed tests can observe any of this because they only run the self-mirroring fake.","severity":"HIGH","better_approach":"Commit a real, unedited `socketfilterfw --listapps` capture from the target machine (header, blank line, and several entries including one path with spaces) as a golden fixture and drive the parser test from it, so real-format drift is actually caught. Make the parser tolerant: skip blank lines, scan for entry lines by pattern instead of assuming rigid index-1 pairing, and normalize surrounding whitespace before the exact-path compare. Close the fail-open seam in mtfc_cleanup_rule: because after-add already proved the rule present (count==1), never clear the ledger on a mere count==0 read — require an observed present→absent transition (or a successful `--remove` against a rule the run actually saw) before declaring verified cleanup, so an inventory mis-parse fails closed (retain ledger, exit 90) rather than silently leaking. Also confirm `--listapps` runs unprivileged on the target, since the helper invokes it without sudo while all mutations use `sudo -n`."}]}

findings.title: Firewall-inventory parser is validated only by a fake reverse-engineered from itself; real `socketfilterfw --listapps` formatting can break it (fail-closed) or defeat cleanup verification (fail-open)
findings.evidence: Every safety property of the helper depends on one strict parser in scripts/macos/with-temporary-firewall-rule.sh:146-180. It requires lines[0] to be the header (155) and then pairs entries starting at `index = 1` (160-171) via `re.fullmatch(r"\s*[0-9]+\s+:\s(.*?)\s*", lines[index])` (162) with NO tolerance for a blank line, a different colon-spacing, or spaces inside `( Allow … )` (165-166). `--listapps` output is fed in raw and unprivileged (line 138). A parse failure becomes a non-zero return (181-182), which the pre-add gate turns into `exit 65` (360-363) and the post-add gate into `exit 71` (452-455). Cleanup (mtfc_cleanup_rule, 374-384) infers rule *absence* from this same parser: `if [[ "$mtfc_inventory_count" -gt 0 ]]` skips `--remove`, then `after-remove` count 0 clears the ledger and reports `cleanup_verified=true` (386-423). The tests never exercise the real tool: the fake in scripts/macos/test-with-temporary-firewall-rule.sh:66-73 emits the header immediately followed by entries with exactly one space after the colon and `(Allow incoming connections)` with no inner spaces — i.e. authored to match the parser. The only real capture in the repo, docs/bench/end-to-end-transfer-latency-2026-07-23/raw/actual-preflight-local.txt:3-4, is a grep excerpt showing just the matching entry+status and NOT the header/blank-line region; a repo-wide search finds no full `--listapps` dump. CI (.github/workflows/ci.yml:47-48) and scripts/macos/run-blit-tests.sh:36-37 run only the fake-backed self-test.
findings.predicted_failure: On real macOS hardware, `socketfilterfw --listapps` commonly prints a blank line after `Total number of apps = N`. The parser reaches lines[1]=="", `re.fullmatch(entry_regex, "")` fails, Python raises `SystemExit("malformed firewall inventory entry")`, `mtfc_capture_inventory` returns 1, and the pre-add check dies with `exit 65 ("cannot parse the pre-add firewall inventory")` — the daemon is never admitted and the OTP-12 acceptance run that docs/plan/OTP12_ACCEPTANCE_RUN.md now gates on this wrapper is blocked. The same brittleness applies to any cross-version difference in colon spacing or `( Allow … )` paren spacing. Worst case, if a real-format difference lets an entry parse but not string-equal the canonical path (an under-count of a present rule), cleanup skips `--remove`, reads count 0, clears the durable ledger, and writes `cleanup_verified=true` while the rule is still live — reintroducing precisely the believed-absent-but-actually-present defect this tool was built to eliminate (docs/plan/MACOS_TEST_FIREWALL_CLEANUP.md:19-33). None of the committed tests can observe any of this because they only run the self-mirroring fake.
findings.better_approach: Commit a real, unedited `socketfilterfw --listapps` capture from the target machine (header, blank line, and several entries including one path with spaces) as a golden fixture and drive the parser test from it, so real-format drift is actually caught. Make the parser tolerant: skip blank lines, scan for entry lines by pattern instead of assuming rigid index-1 pairing, and normalize surrounding whitespace before the exact-path compare. Close the fail-open seam in mtfc_cleanup_rule: because after-add already proved the rule present (count==1), never clear the ledger on a mere count==0 read — require an observed present→absent transition (or a successful `--remove` against a rule the run actually saw) before declaring verified cleanup, so an inventory mis-parse fails closed (retain ledger, exit 90) rather than silently leaking. Also confirm `--listapps` runs unprivileged on the target, since the helper invokes it without sudo while all mutations use `sudo -n`.
